3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Al-Shoaibi, A. A., Leong, A. W., Zamora, G., Testa, A., Ganson, K. T., & Baker, F. C. (2024). Screen time and mental health: a prospective analysis of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. BMC Public Health, 24, 2686.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used longitudinal data from baseline to Year 2 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (4.0 release). Among 11,875 participants, 2,337 had missing data for total screen time and confounders, especially in Year 2, leaving 9,538 participants for the current analysis.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Total screen time and six screen time subtypes (TV/movies, videos/YouTube, video games, texting, video chat, social media) at baseline
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (weighted average: (weekday x 5 + weekend x 2)/7)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was obtained from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Participants were asked to answer questions about the number of hours per weekday/weekend day they spent on six different screen modalities. Total screen time was calculated separately for weekdays and weekend days, based on a previously validated measure. The weighted average of total screen time was reported as a continuous variable.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: CBCL DSM-oriented scale t-scores (depressive, anxiety, somatic, ADHD, ODD, conduct symptoms) from Year 1 and Year 2
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The CBCL is a screening tool consisting of 112 items asking a parent/caretaker about multiple behavioral, emotional, and mental health symptoms in children and adolescents aged 4 to 18 years. The CBCL included six DSM-oriented scales, including depressive, anxiety, somatic, attention-deficit/hyperactivity, oppositional defiant, and conduct symptoms. T-scores were calculated based on the CBCL scoring rubric.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Mixed-effects models with random effects for repeated CBCL measures at Year 1 and Year 2
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, data collection period (pre/during COVID), study site, baseline CBCL DSM-oriented scale t-scores, date of CBCL administration
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used mixed-effects models with random effects to assess the association of baseline screen time with each CBCL DSM-oriented scale. In Model 2, the outcomes were CBCL DSM-oriented scale t-scores from Year 1 and Year 2, adjusted for baseline CBCL DSM-oriented scale t-scores and the following confounders at baseline. We tested for effect modification by sex and race/ethnicity. Propensity weights based on the American Community Survey from the ABCD Study were applied.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Total screen time: 0.10 (0.06, 0.13), p<0.001 for depressive symptoms
Total screen time: 0.07 (0.03, 0.10), p=0.011 for conduct symptoms
Total screen time: 0.06 (0.01, 0.11), p=0.026 for somatic symptoms
Total screen time: 0.06 (0.01, 0.10), p=0.013 for attention-deficit/hyperactivity symptoms
Total screen time: 0.05 (0.01, 0.09), p=0.036 for anxiety symptoms (Model 2 adjusted)
Total screen time: 0.04 (0.01, 0.07) for ODD symptoms

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Screen time is prospectively associated with a range of mental health symptoms, especially depressive symptoms, though effect sizes are small. The effect sizes were relatively small.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Both the abstract and conclusion explicitly state effect sizes are small. Discussion includes nuanced discussion of whether small effects are meaningful, citing competing perspectives.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These findings can help to inform developmentally appropriate guidance related to screen use, especially for adolescents and their parents. The American Academy of Pediatrics advocates for a Family Media Use plan for children 5 to 18 years old, which could be individualized for adolescents based on some of the associations noted in the current study.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: others have suggested that the consequences of screen time at a population level are likely meaningful despite small effect sizes
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: acting on modifiable behaviors in early adolescence could be protective
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: these effect sizes per hour of screen time are similar in magnitude to the effect sizes previously reported on screen time and nutrition
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges small effect sizes in both the abstract and body text. However, it deploys several rhetorical strategies to argue the findings are still meaningful: the population-level importance argument, the per-unit framing suggesting effects accumulate with more hours, comparison to other lifestyle factors (nutrition), and the modifiable behavior framing. It explicitly cites both sides of the debate on whether small effects matter but ultimately recommends guidance on screen use and references AAP Family Media Use Plans.
